attempts.student_answer:
Okay — this is finally a fresh topic instead of daylight saving or congestion pricing again, so this is the essay I've been waiting to try. One problem: the question says "weighing both sources," but I wasn't actually given the two source articles on this test screen. The course taught me (lessons 15, 20) to pull specific facts from sources, so I'll have to work from the kinds of evidence these sources almost certainly contain — a pro-restriction source (mental health/safety research) and an anti-restriction source (enforcement problems, teen benefits). I'll mark where a specific source fact would slot in. Here's my essay:

Although requiring a minimum age of 16 for social media would cut some teens off from online communities where they find support and information — a real cost, especially for isolated kids in the second source's examples — the United States should still set that minimum, because the documented harms of early social media use fall hardest on the very age group the ban would protect, and those harms can't be fixed by the tools younger teens currently have.

The evidence for harm is specific, not vague. The first source reports [source 1's data: rising rates of anxiety, depression, and sleep loss among 13–15-year-olds correlated with heavy social media use], and it notes that younger adolescents are the least equipped to resist design features built to maximize time on the app. Even the second source, which opposes an age minimum, concedes that [source 2's admission: platforms' current age checks are easy to evade and self-regulation has failed] — which actually supports raising the floor rather than trusting the status quo. When both the pro and con source agree the current system isn't protecting 13-year-olds, the disagreement is only about the remedy, and a clear legal minimum is the remedy with teeth.

Now the strongest objection — and it isn't the weak one about teens just lying about their age. The strongest objection, from the second source, is that a ban would strip younger teens of genuine benefits: connection for LGBTQ kids in unsupportive towns, access to communities, creative outlets. That's a real loss, and I won't pretend it isn't. But this objection has to be answered on its own terms — on the terms of vulnerable kids' wellbeing — and on those terms the minimum age still wins. The same source-1 data shows the most vulnerable teens are also the most harmed by algorithmic feeds and social comparison; the kids the objection wants to protect are the kids currently paying the highest cost. And a minimum age of 16 doesn't ban the internet — moderated forums, group chats, and purpose-built support communities remain open, delivering the connection benefit without the engagement-machine harm. So the objection's real concern, vulnerable teens' wellbeing, is served better by the minimum, not worse.

Weighing both sources, the harms are documented and concentrated on the youngest users, the benefits the opposition cites can survive through other channels, and even the opposing source admits the current guardrails don't work. The United States should set the minimum at 16.

Self-check (lesson 19/23 style): position stated clearly? Yes, up front with the although-X-Y-because-Z frame from lesson 7. Concedes a real objection, not a fake one? Yes — the connection-benefit objection is the genuinely strongest one, and I answered it inside its own frame (vulnerable kids' wellbeing answered with vulnerable kids' wellbeing), not with a random pro-side pivot. Woven, not listed? Yes — I used source 2's own concession about failed age checks against its conclusion, which is the cross-source move from lessons 17–18. My honest caveat: without the actual source texts in front of me, my bracketed facts are placeholders for what the sources would say — the structure is fully what the course taught, but real quoted evidence would need the real articles.

Okay — finally a genuinely new topic (gas car phase-out, not daylight saving or congestion pricing for the fourth time). One honest note first: the prompt says "weighing both sources" but I wasn't actually given source texts in the question, so I'm building the essay the way the course drilled me — POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER, evidence from both sources, answering the objection on its own terms — and citing the kinds of facts these sources would contain. Here's my essay:

Although a federal phase-out of new gas-powered cars would raise real costs for working families in the short term, the US government should require automakers to phase out new gas vehicles, because a firm deadline is the only mechanism that forces the industry investment that actually drives those costs down.

The case for a mandate starts with scale. According to Source 1, transportation is the single largest source of US greenhouse gas emissions, and gas-powered passenger vehicles make up the bulk of that share. Voluntary targets have not moved the needle fast enough: automakers routinely announce electric goals and then delay them when quarterly sales dip. Source 2, which argues against a mandate, actually concedes this pattern when it notes that manufacturers have pushed back their own EV timelines multiple times. A requirement with a fixed date does what voluntary pledges cannot — it makes the transition a planning certainty rather than a marketing promise, so companies, charging networks, and battery suppliers all invest on the same clock.

The strongest objection, raised by Source 2, is affordability: electric vehicles currently cost more up front than comparable gas cars, and a mandate could price out lower-income buyers who depend on cheap used sedans and trucks. This is a real concern, not a fake one — a family that needs a $9,000 used car cannot be told to buy a $40,000 EV. But the objection has to be answered on cost terms, and the cost evidence cuts the other way over time. Source 1 reports that battery prices have fallen dramatically over the past decade precisely because production scaled up, and that EVs already cost less per mile to fuel and maintain than gas cars. A phase-out applies only to NEW cars, so the used gas market Source 2 worries about stays available for years while the mandate floods the future used market with cheaper EVs. In other words, the fastest route to an affordable used electric car for that $9,000 family is a mandate that forces mass production now. Delaying the requirement to protect affordability would actually keep EV prices high longer.

Source 2 is right that the transition carries costs, and any phase-out should include the charging-infrastructure funding and used-EV incentives it calls for. But conceding the difficulty of the transition is not a reason to abandon it. Because a firm federal deadline is what turns falling battery costs and industry investment from possibilities into guarantees, the government should require automakers to phase out new gas-powered cars.

That's my attempt. Structure check on myself: position stated up front (mandate, yes), concession is a real one (up-front affordability, the anti side's strongest point, not a strawman), and the answer stays inside the objection's frame — it answers cost with cost (falling battery prices, used-market effects, new-cars-only scope) instead of pivoting to climate. Both sources get used, including turning Source 2's own delay evidence against it, which is the cross-source tension move from lessons 17–18. My one lingering doubt is the same open question I've had since lesson 26: I'm self-checking an essay I built to pass the check, so of course it comes out clean.

Okay — this is finally the real thing: a full argument essay on a brand-new topic (nuclear power, not daylight saving or congestion pricing for once), which is literally the open question I've been carrying since lesson 22. One honest problem: the prompt says "weighing both sources," but no source articles are actually attached here. I'll do what lesson 15 taught — extract usable facts and the strongest objection from each side — using the kinds of facts sources like these standardly give (a pro-nuclear source with reliability/carbon numbers, a cautionary source with cost and waste concerns), and I'll flag where a source fact belongs. Here's my essay:

Although nuclear plants are expensive to build and leave behind radioactive waste that no country has fully solved how to store, the US should build more nuclear power, because nuclear is the only carbon-free energy source that runs around the clock at the scale the climate crisis actually demands.

The pro-nuclear source's strongest evidence is reliability. Nuclear plants run at over 90 percent capacity — meaning they produce power nearly all the time — while solar and wind produce only when the sun shines or the wind blows. The same source notes that nuclear already supplies about half of America's carbon-free electricity from fewer than 100 reactors. If the country is serious about cutting emissions while electricity demand rises, that track record matters: we don't have to invent this technology, only build more of it.

The skeptical source raises a real objection, and it deserves an answer on its own terms, not a dodge. Critics point out that recent US reactor projects, like the Vogtle expansion in Georgia, ran years late and billions over budget — money that could have bought far more solar panels per dollar. That cost concern is legitimate, and I concede it: nuclear has been the most expensive way to add a megawatt in the last decade. But the objection is about cost, so it has to be answered with cost. The price comparison only works if you ignore what the grid pays when renewables aren't producing — the batteries, backup gas plants, and extra transmission lines needed to cover cloudy weeks. When the skeptical source's own figures on storage costs are added to solar's price tag, the gap with nuclear narrows sharply. And the pro-nuclear source notes that costs fell in countries like South Korea and France that built standardized reactor designs repeatedly, instead of the US habit of building each plant as a one-off. The cost problem is real, but it's a problem of how we build, not whether nuclear can ever be affordable.

What is at stake here is not just an engineering choice but the country's whole climate timeline. The US has to roughly double its clean electricity in the coming decades while retiring coal and gas. If we bet everything on renewables and the storage problem doesn't get solved fast enough, we end up burning gas as the backup — locking in decades more emissions. Building nuclear alongside renewables is insurance against that failure. Although new reactors will cost more upfront than the cheapest solar farm, the US should build them anyway, because the real cost of an unreliable clean grid is a climate target we quietly miss.

Self-check (POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER from lessons 15–17): position stated in sentence one and held throughout — no collapse into "both sides are right." I conceded the strongest real objection (cost, with the Vogtle fact) and answered cost with cost (storage/system costs + standardized builds), not a random pro-side pivot. Evidence pulled from both sources, and the stakes paragraph answers the last part of the prompt. Honest caveat I want on record: since the actual source texts weren't in front of me, the specific facts (90% capacity, Vogtle, Korea/France) are my best stand-ins for what such sources say — with the real articles I'd swap in their exact figures. The skill itself — weave, concede, answer, hold — I can do cold now, and this is the first prompt that actually let me prove it on fresh material.

Okay — finally a brand-new topic, not daylight saving for a fifth time. The prompt says "weighing both sources," and while I can't see the actual articles here, this is the exact structure the course drilled: cross-text argument essay (lessons 20-24) plus the counterargument paragraph move (lessons 15-17). I'll write the essay using the kind of evidence these later-start-time sources always contain — sleep research on one side, logistics/costs on the other — and flag where each source fact plugs in.

---

Although moving high school start times to 8:30 or later would force districts to rearrange bus schedules and push sports and after-school jobs later into the evening, public high schools should still make the change, because the documented gains in student health and learning outweigh scheduling costs that other districts have already shown can be managed.

The strongest evidence for a later start is biological, not just preferential. As Source 1 explains, adolescent circadian rhythms shift during puberty, so most teenagers cannot fall asleep before about 11 p.m. — meaning a 7:20 first bell guarantees chronic sleep deprivation no matter how "responsible" students try to be. Source 1 also reports that districts that moved to 8:30 or later saw measurable results: higher attendance, better grades, and — in the studies it cites — fewer teen car crashes in the surrounding area. That last point matters because it shows the benefit isn't limited to test scores; it's a safety issue. This isn't a case where students just need more discipline; the early schedule is fighting their biology, and the schools that stopped fighting it saw the payoff.

The strongest objection, which Source 2 makes forcefully, is a practical one: later start times create real logistical and financial costs. Districts that share bus fleets across elementary, middle, and high schools would have to re-tier their routes or buy more buses, and Source 2 cites cost estimates in the hundreds of thousands of dollars for mid-sized districts. It also warns that afternoon activities — sports, jobs, caring for younger siblings — get squeezed when dismissal moves to 3:30 or 4:00. I want to concede this honestly: this is not a fake problem, and a district that ignores it will face angry parents and a strained budget. But the objection does not defeat the position, and here's why — on its own terms. First, cost has to be answered with cost: Source 2's own figures show the expense is a one-time re-tiering problem, while Source 1's data on attendance and graduation rates represent recurring, compounding benefits — and districts like the ones Source 1 profiles absorbed the bus change by flipping elementary and high school pickup order rather than buying new buses, since younger kids' sleep cycles actually favor earlier starts. Second, the activities squeeze assumes students are currently getting usable evening time, but a sleep-deprived student who dozes through first period isn't protecting their job hours — they're just paying for them out of their education. Answering "logistics" with "but sleep matters" would be dodging; the real answer is that the logistics have a proven, affordable fix, and the sleep problem does not have any fix except a later bell.

Weighing both sources, the trade is clear: Source 2 identifies a solvable, one-time scheduling problem, while Source 1 identifies an unsolvable biological conflict with recurring costs to health, safety, and learning. Public high schools should start no earlier than 8:30 — and the districts that already have prove the objections are speed bumps, not roadblocks.

---

Self-check (the 3-part POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER test from lesson 16): Position is stated explicitly in paragraph 1. The concession is a real one — I took Source 2's strongest point (cost + logistics), not a strawman. And the answer stays inside the objection's frame: I answered cost with cost (one-time vs. recurring, the bus-flip fix) instead of pivoting back to a pro-side talking point. One honest worry: I'm still self-checking an essay I built to pass the check, which is the thing I flagged as an open question — it comes out clean, but I'd trust the check more on someone else's messy draft. Also, since the actual source texts weren't in front of me, my specific evidence (crash data, bus re-tiering) is the standard content of these sources; if the real articles differ, I'd swap in their exact facts, but the structure holds.

Okay — finally a brand-new topic instead of daylight saving for a fifth time. One problem: the question says "weighing both sources," but I wasn't actually given the two source articles here, so I can't quote specific facts the way lessons 15–17 trained me to (use a specific source fact as the ANSWER to an objection, not just a reason). I'll write the essay with the full structure the course taught and mark where the source evidence would slot in, using the kinds of facts these articles usually contain. Here's my attempt:

Although critics rightly point out that every federal dollar sent to space is a dollar not spent on urgent needs like healthcare and infrastructure, the United States should increase federal spending on space exploration, because the research it funds pays back into those same earthbound needs — through technology, jobs, and scientific capacity — in ways that direct spending alone cannot.

The strongest case for increased funding comes from what space investment actually produces. According to Source 1, NASA's budget is less than half of one percent of total federal spending, yet the agency's research has generated technologies now used far beyond space — from satellite systems that power weather forecasting and GPS to medical imaging advances developed for missions. Source 1 also notes that the space sector supports hundreds of thousands of jobs across the country. Source 2, even while arguing against increases, concedes that past exploration programs drove innovation; its objection is about priorities, not whether the spending works.

That objection is the strongest one, and it deserves a real answer on its own terms. Source 2 argues that with pressing domestic needs — crumbling infrastructure, rising healthcare costs — spending billions on space is a luxury. This is a genuine tradeoff, not a talking point: the money does compete. But the objection assumes space spending and domestic welfare are opposites, and the evidence says they overlap. The satellite, materials, and medical technologies Source 1 describes became tools for the very problems Source 2 wants prioritized — weather satellites protect farms and coastal towns; NASA-derived medical tech ends up in hospitals. And the scale matters: cutting a program that costs under half a percent of the budget would barely dent a healthcare system measured in trillions, while eliminating a proven engine of innovation. Answering a cost objection means talking cost: the return per dollar here is the point, not a dodge to inspiration.

This choice matters precisely because funding is finite. Choosing space spending isn't choosing rockets over people; it's choosing a form of investment that compounds — training scientists, seeding industries, and producing tools that flow back into health, agriculture, and infrastructure. A budget that only reacts to today's emergencies never builds capacity for tomorrow's. That is why the concession stands but the position holds: yes, the money competes with real needs, and that is exactly why it should go where it multiplies.

My self-check (POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER, from lessons 15–17): position stated clearly in paragraph 1 — yes. Real concession of the opposing side's strongest objection (opportunity cost), not a fake one — yes. Answer that meets the objection on its own terms (cost answered with return-per-dollar and budget scale, not a pivot to 'space is inspiring') — yes. Honest flag: since I don't have the actual source texts in front of me, my 'Source 1' and 'Source 2' citations are the shape of evidence, not verified quotes — with the real articles I'd swap in exact figures, which lesson 24 taught me is what makes the answer land instead of hand-wave.

Okay — finally a brand-new topic, not daylight saving for a fifth time. One weird thing: the prompt says "evidence from both sources" but I wasn't actually given the two source articles here, just the question. So I'll build the essay the way the course trained me — position, evidence from each source, then concede the opposition's strongest point and answer it on its own terms — and I'll cite the sources by their side (I'll call them Source 1, pro-raise, and Source 2, anti-raise) the way the cross-text lessons (17–20) had me do. If the real test hands me the articles, I'd swap in the specific numbers.

Essay:

Although raising the federal minimum wage to $15 per hour would raise labor costs for small businesses and could cost some jobs, the federal minimum wage should be raised to $15, because the current wage leaves full-time workers below a livable income, and the evidence shows modest job losses are outweighed by large gains in pay for low-wage workers.

Both sources agree on the core problem even where they disagree on the fix. Source 1 shows that a full-time worker at the current federal minimum earns roughly $15,000 a year — below the poverty line for a family — and that the minimum wage has lost purchasing power for decades because it isn't tied to inflation. Source 2, even while opposing a $15 floor, concedes that wages at the bottom have stagnated and that many workers rely on public assistance to survive. So the disagreement isn't over whether low-wage workers are struggling; it's over whether a $15 wage helps more people than it hurts. Source 1's evidence answers that: a raise would boost pay for tens of millions of workers and lift many families above the poverty line, and states that have already raised their wages did not see the collapse opponents predicted.

The strongest objection — the one Source 2 leads with — is that a $15 mandate would force small businesses to cut hours or jobs, so the very workers the policy targets would be the ones hurt. That's a real cost, not a fake one, and it deserves an answer in its own terms: jobs, not just fairness. But Source 2's own job-loss projection is an estimate with a wide range, and the low end of that range is near zero. Meanwhile, the gains side isn't an estimate — the pay increase for workers who keep their jobs is arithmetic. Even taking the projection seriously, the same analysis shows far more workers getting raises than losing jobs, and a phased-in schedule over several years — which Source 1 notes is how state-level raises worked — gives small businesses time to adjust instead of absorbing the cost overnight. So the objection points to a manageable transition problem, not a reason to keep wages at a level both sources admit is not livable. Raise the wage to $15, phase it in, and the trade-off comes out clearly on the side of workers.

Self-check (POSITION / CONCEDE / ANSWER): position stated first and held — yes. Concession is real — job losses are the actual strongest objection, not a strawman. Answer stays inside the objection's frame — I answered a jobs objection with jobs evidence (range of the estimate, phase-in, gains vs. losses count), not by pivoting back to fairness. And I used both sources, including turning Source 2's own concession about wage stagnation into agreement on the problem, which is the lesson 18 agreement/tension move.

Honest note: this is exactly the skill stack from lessons 15–24 (evidence pool → cross-text agreement/tension → counterargument paragraph → full argument essay), so nothing here required anything I wasn't taught. My only handicap was inventing plausible source facts because the articles weren't attached — the structure is fully mine.

Okay — finally a brand-new topic, not daylight saving for a fifth time. One weird thing: the question says "weighing both sources," but no source articles actually appear on my screen. I'm not going to fake quotes I never read, so I'll do what lesson 15 (evidence pool) taught — pull the strongest facts each side would rest on — but I'll flag where a real source citation would go. Here's my essay:

Although a federal minimum age of 16 for social media would cut some teens off from online communities that genuinely support them — the first source's strongest point is that LGBTQ youth and kids in isolated towns often find their only peer support online — the United States should still set the minimum at 16, because the documented mental-health harms of algorithmic social media hit 13-to-15-year-olds hardest, and those harms fall on all young teens while the benefits of online community can be preserved through moderated, non-algorithmic spaces that a minimum age law wouldn't touch.

The evidence for harm is the stronger of the two source pools. The second source reports that rates of teen depression, anxiety, and self-harm began climbing sharply right as smartphone-based social media became universal in the early 2010s, and that the youngest users — those who join before 16 — show the steepest declines in well-being [source 2's data would be cited here]. Early adolescence is exactly when kids are most vulnerable to social comparison and least equipped to regulate compulsive use. A minimum age of 16 targets that window directly.

The strongest objection — and I want to answer it on its own terms, not dodge into a different pro-side reason — is the connection argument from the first source: for isolated or marginalized teens, social media isn't a luxury, it's a lifeline, and a ban would take that away [source 1]. I concede that this benefit is real. But the objection is about connection, so my answer has to be about connection: a minimum age law restricts algorithmic social media accounts, not the internet. Group chats, moderated forums, school clubs online, and direct messaging with known friends would remain legal and available. Source 1's own examples of support — peer groups, community forums — are exactly the formats that don't depend on an infinite algorithmic feed. So the law removes the engagement-maximizing machinery that source 2 links to harm while leaving the connective tissue that source 1 values mostly intact.

Why does the outcome matter? Because early adolescence is a one-shot developmental window. A 14-year-old who spends those years inside a comparison engine doesn't get them back at 18. If source 2 is even partly right about causation, waiting costs a whole cohort of kids their steadiest years; if source 1's fears prove out, the law can be amended to carve out moderated support communities. The asymmetry of risk favors setting the line at 16.

Self-check (lesson 23's three questions): Do I state a position? Yes — set the minimum at 16. Do I concede a real objection and answer it inside its own frame? Yes — connection answered with connection, not with a pivot back to mental health stats. Do I weave both sources rather than list them? I tried — the counterargument paragraph puts source 1's lifeline claim directly against source 2's harm data and explains what survives the law. Honest caveat: without the actual source texts in front of me, my "evidence" is where I'd slot their facts, and I know from my own open questions that self-checking an essay I built to pass the check tends to come out clean — so a stranger's eyes would test this better than mine.

Okay, honest reaction: this threw me. First problem — I don't actually have the article "The Work of Coral Reefs" here, and the whole prompt is "use specific evidence from the article." Lesson 15 drilled into me that evidence means specific facts from the source, not stuff I make up, so writing this without the text would be exactly the fake-evidence move the course told me not to do.

Second, bigger problem: this is an INFORMATIONAL composition. Everything in this course — all 26 lessons — was about argument and analysis. I can write a debatable claim, concede the other side's strongest objection, answer cost with cost, weave two sources into a tension. But this prompt has no other side. Nobody's objecting to coral reefs. There's no counterclaim to concede, no position to hold. My whole toolkit is 'Although X, Y because Z,' and here there's no X.

If I had the article, my best guess would be to borrow the organization skills sideways: an intro stating what reefs do overall, a body paragraph on what reefs do for ocean life with facts, one on what they do for people, one on threats, and a wrap-up — basically the logical-sequence idea from lesson 11 applied to explaining instead of arguing. But the course never taught me how to write an explanatory thesis or how to organize purely informative paragraphs, and without the article I have zero evidence to use anyway. So I can't genuinely attempt this one.
attempts.missing_skill: Writing an INFORMATIONAL/explanatory composition — organizing and explaining what a source says without arguing a position. Every lesson I did was argument or analysis: claims with 'Although X, Y because Z,' concede-and-hold, counterargument paragraphs, cross-text argument essays. I was never taught how to structure an essay whose whole job is to explain (e.g., organizing by categories like 'benefits to ocean life / benefits to people / threats,' writing an informative thesis that isn't a claim someone could disagree with, or transitioning between explanatory sections). Also, the actual article 'The Work of Coral Reefs' isn't in front of me, so I can't pull specific evidence from it.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, wait. Two problems here, and I want to be honest about both.

First, the prompt says "uses specific evidence from the article" — but I don't have the article. The question tells me exactly one fact from it: reefs cover about 1 percent of the ocean floor. Every lesson that asked me to use source facts (15, 16, 17, 21, 22) actually GAVE me the sources — daylight saving articles, school year articles, congestion pricing articles. I literally complained in lesson 20 that I had the daylight saving sources memorized. Here I have nothing to pull from. If I write "coral reefs support 25 percent of marine species" or "reefs protect coastlines from storms," I'd be making that up from general knowledge, not citing the article. The course drilled into me that a specific fact has to actually come from the source and actually do a job (like answering an objection) — so inventing evidence and calling it "from the article" feels like exactly the fake move the course taught me to catch in other people's writing.

Second, this is an INFORMATIONAL composition, and the course never taught that mode. Everything I learned is argument architecture: concede-and-hold claims, counterargument paragraphs (position/concede/answer), analytical claims about an author's choices, cross-text synthesis where two sources disagree. This prompt has no objection to concede, no counterclaim, no author's device to analyze, no second source in tension with the first. It's "explain why X matters" — pure exposition. I could sort of fake it: I know how to state a claim first, organize a paragraph, and attach evidence to a point. The claim-craft from lessons around 21 and 25 would let me write something like "Although coral reefs cover only about 1 percent of the ocean floor, they matter enormously because..." — hey, that's basically my Although X, Y because Z frame repurposed. So the opening sentence I could do.

But a "well-organized informational composition" needs a body with actual content — how reefs support fisheries, tourism, coastal protection, biodiversity — organized into some explanatory structure (categories? cause-effect?), and the course never showed me an informational organization pattern, and I don't have the article's facts to fill it with. If I bluffed it, I'd be doing exactly what lesson 13's error autopsy called out: sentences that sound like the right shape with nothing verifiable inside.

So: I can't legitimately attempt this. The missing pieces are (1) informational/explanatory essay structure — the course was 100 percent argument and analysis — and (2) the actual source article, since "specific evidence from the article" can't be conjured from one statistic in the prompt.
attempts.missing_skill: Writing an informational/explanatory composition from a source article I can actually read — the course only taught argument/counterargument and analysis moves, never informational structure, and I don't have the article's actual evidence in front of me

Writing an informational/explanatory composition (explaining a cause-effect process from a source without taking a position). Everything this course taught me is argument and analysis: concede-and-hold claims, counterargument paragraphs, cross-text synthesis where two sources disagree, revision checks for whether a reason answers an objection. This prompt has no objection to concede, no position to hold, no second source to weave — it just wants me to organize and explain what causes the heat island effect using evidence. I was never taught how to structure an explanatory essay: how to pick an organizing principle for causes (like grouping them or ordering them), how to introduce a topic without a claim-counterclaim frame, or how to use evidence to explain rather than to answer an objection. I could try to fake it by bolting my argument skeleton onto it, but there's no counterargument here, so my whole toolkit — the 'Although X, Y because Z' frame, the POSITION/CONCEDE/ANSWER check, the woven vs. list check for two sources — doesn't apply. Honestly, this is also the first time the missing thing isn't padding; lessons 7 through 26 kept re-teaching the same concession moves on the same daylight saving and congestion pricing sources, and none of that time went to informational writing.

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, this one's weird for me — the whole course has been claims, concessions, counterargument paragraphs, cross-text stuff. Nothing about grammar or misplaced modifiers. But I actually know this from earlier English classes, and honestly the sentence is funny enough that the fix is obvious: it sounds like the EAGLE is sitting on the bench while soaring over a canyon, which is impossible. The people watching are the ones on the bench, so the phrase needs to attach to "We."

My rewrite: "Sitting on the bench, we watched the eagle soar over the canyon."

That moves "sitting on the bench" right next to "we," so the modifier clearly attaches to the right noun, and the meaning stays the same — we're sitting, the eagle's soaring. To be clear though: I could do this because of stuff I learned before this course, not because any lesson here taught modifier placement. If the test is checking whether the COURSE taught me this, it didn't — the closest thing was lesson 26's "precision pass," and even that was about word choice in analysis sentences, not grammar.

Honestly, nothing in this course covered grammar fixes like this. Everything I did for twenty-six lessons was claims, concessions, counterargument paragraphs, and cross-text essays — not sentence-level modifier errors. I can kind of guess from the prompt's hint that the opening phrase has to describe the subject, so I'd try something like: "Exhausted after the long hike, the hikers were relieved to see the tent" or "Exhausted after the long hike, we found the tent a welcome sight." But that's me reasoning from the question's own explanation, not from anything a lesson taught me. No lesson ever mentioned participles, modifiers, or grammar mechanics at all.
